transcript.text: Hey, how's it going? Living in Boulder V-Log, August 1st, 2019, Thursday at 4.01 p.m.
I didn't do a video yesterday because I just, yeah, I got carried away.
And then I was going to sleep at like 5.30 or something or 6.
And I was like, oh, the video, should I do it?
But I thought, I figured I was too tired.
I probably should have done it, though.
I want to keep my consistency.
But whatever.
I think that's like the first time I can remember off the top of my head that I did that.
So I've been here working on the maps of Boulder.
But then there's an 83 that I can build off the directory, which I think I'm going to do.
So how many is that?
I don't know how many that is.
But I started and I did 64 because that was easy.
And then because I was just including everything in the map on my web page.
And then I think I did 68, and then I was working on 80.
But I had to write the names, and that was really tedious.
So I decided to skip that, but I did the roads on it.
And so then I'm skipping 83 because I don't have the directory yet.
You have to – they don't put it on the internet.
I found it.
If you Google 1883 Boulder, Colorado directory, Google Books has it, but just the first nine pages.
And there are like 36 pages or something.
I can't remember.
Or 18 pages.
But in order to – you can't see them.
You have to – and there's no way to be able to see them.
You have to get the book online.
So that's like mental illness and sanity right there.
They have the technology to just show me all 18 pages.
So there's one library that has it, the Pikes Peak Library.
So I'm going to call them and see if I can like become a member and get the book.
But I don't want like a hardcover book.
I need a digital – maybe I can become a member or whatever and check it out to unlock it, to digitally see it.
But, yeah, or maybe I'll just skip it.
But, yeah, I started it like in a weird way that didn't make sense on my website.
I think I'll just have to ditch that.
So, yeah, then I think I was working – I did the roads of 87 or 98 or maybe not.
Maybe I skipped to 2015.
I'm really high right now.
And so, yeah, I was working on most of yesterday on 1915.
That's the most detailed map that there is of Boulder from the past, which was magical because when I started working on it, it was August of 2014.
I know that because that's when I put the photos on Flickr, the date stamp was on there.
And so, yeah, exactly 100 years later, I was building it from when the map was.
So, right now I'm just doing touch-ups.
I think what happened was this was, I think, the second major project that I started that was new since I moved to Boulder.
I was in Lafayette for six months or something like that, and then I moved here, and when I moved here, all of a sudden, I was thinking I was working on my timeline.
But all of a sudden, I looked out, and I saw the flight, and I was like, oh, Boulder.
So I decided I was going to become an athlete, and that's when I built a bike racer levels page.
But then I got, like, kind of burnt out on it because I was working on it forever.
It got way more advanced than I ever thought it was.
And so I just kind of – and then that's when I noticed about Boulder.
Or maybe I just randomly had the idea.
But I went down, and I had to – I had to actually physically go down to the Carnegie Library and use their portable scanner that they have to get the giant image of 1915.
It's a huge map, like six feet by eight feet or something like that.

And I had to go in there and just, like, hold the thing over, really – like, put it on a bunch of tables, like, put tables together, and then scan it really slowly in strips, and then paste the strips together.
And, yeah, because that map's just not online.
At least it wasn't then.
So that's what I used to build Boulder off of.
But it was too big for my computer to open.
So I had to have, like, two computers.
I think I had an older computer then.
So one computer would just hold the image open, and I would look at it, and then the other computer would do the Google Earth stuff.
Because it was, like, 55 megabytes, which was too big for the computer to handle then.
And it wouldn't, of course, open it up on Google Earth.
But now, with my new Macintosh, yeah, it's just like another image.
It just opens on Google Earth.
But it only shows the left side because I guess it's too big to show the whole thing.
So it started on the left, and then it just, when it got tired, it just forgot about it.
But Boulder is on the left, and the right side is just a bunch of land.
So it's not really even that important.
So it worked out great.
So, yeah, it's just like a normal picture.
I mean, it takes longer to appear, like a few seconds.
But, yeah, that's pretty cool.
My computer can totally handle it.
It makes it so much easier.
You just click it on, and it superimposes.
And so that's what I did.
But then that really drug out because I decided to do all those other maps, too.
And then I did the images.
And then I did – actually, I started with Caribou.
Yeah, that's how I did.
I suddenly got fascinated with Caribou.
I built Caribou.
And then I said, wait, if I'm doing Caribou, I need to do Boulder.
So – but then, yeah, and then I did all the – like started with the mines and ghost towns and then Boulder Rome.
And it got out of control.
And so – oh, no, no, not Boulder Rome.
Boulder Rome came later.
But it got out of control.
And then I think – but I was just about to finish it and stuff because I remember when I was working on it, I said, oh, this is so exciting.
It's going to be the first – this first project that I finish in the same go that I started.
But that turned out not to be the case because I was just like happenstanced upon a map.
It was on the Rumsey map website.
And I just happenstanced upon a map of Rome that you could put on Google Earth.
That somebody used some fancy coding or whatever to get the map of – well, there was this huge ancient map of Rome that they mapped out
That I didn't even know exists that they recreated.
But then they pasted it onto Google Earth like perfectly.
Like where none of it got warped from the shape and stuff.
They just – it was this different way.
And so I looked at that and said, oh, my God, all you have to do then if you wanted to know what ancient Rome looks like is trace all of this map.
And so I suddenly got really excited about that.
And that's when I abandoned Boulder.
So that's why I've been in Boulder.
And I went on Rome and, of course, I abandoned Rome because that was just way too big of a project.
And I can't remember what replaced Rome.
I've done lots of different random things since then.
But, yeah, so now I'm working on Boulder again.
So when I'm looking at it, I'm kind of at the spot where it was – because I looked at my webpage for it and it was barely started.
And I think that was in the days when I had the slower computers that couldn't handle big pages.
And so I think I, like, wasn't ambitious or I gave up or never got around to it.
But so now what I'm doing is I'm just picking up where I left off on Boulder where I'm just numbering everything and getting it primed.
So all I have to do is copy and paste it onto text because it doesn't paste directly on Dreamweaver for some reason.
But copy and paste it onto, yeah, onto the website on Dreamweaver so everything is labeled, like all the roads and, yeah, even the oil rigs.
There's, like, 190 of them.
And, yeah, so I'm just organizing that now and numbering and placing them right.
So that's going to take a few more days.
But it's super exciting because it's, you know, it's going to come together pretty quickly just in a matter of a few days and then put it on the website.
And then I'll make the realistic version so that I could then make movies of it.
Because right now, you know, I have the lot version where it's all, like, rainbow colored to show all the lots.
But, yeah, so that's what I'm doing right now is, like, in the roads, I have, you know, north, south, east, west.
But I also have roads divided by different locations, you know.
They're special locations.
They're little enclaves with their own little ruds in Boulder.
The University of Colorado, Chautauqua, Addison Subdivision, Valmont.
So, yeah, I'm doing the final one, the University of Colorado.
Just putting them all the roads in their place.
And then I'll, then that's when I'm going to place a number of the east to west roads.
And, uh, and then the alleys.
That'll be interesting to see how I do that.
But, yeah, accounting for everything and then put it on the internet.
Uh, you know, you can download Boulder right now and you can see the steps.
Because I upload it all the time.
So, um, the, both the page and the Google Earth.
Um, but yeah, it's getting better fast.
But, uh, um, yeah, uh, I was listening to, uh, Strabo.
Because I was watching a bunch of videos from History Mystery Channel.
And he mentioned how Strabo said that in the ancient pyramid, they, somebody had a swivel door.
That was waited so a person could just push on it and go in.
I was like, Strabo said that?
So, I was just listening to a bunch of Strabo.
I didn't hear the part where he talked about that.
But, um, it was fun listening to Strabo.
He's from, uh, you know, 2,000 years ago, Rome.
Um, talking about geography and the history of, like, all around.
It's an interesting book.
And, like, more than 1,000 pages, 1,700 pages.
So, um, yeah, I'm working on 2,000, 1,000 pages.
1,500, the big kahuna.
Um, maybe I can finish it today.
I don't know.
I've been sleeping a lot.
I slept a lot.
Oh, and for the last two days, I've been helping my mom in her basement for, like, we were doing a big project, getting out stuff.
Um, we got out a bunch of these really nice plates.
And I'm going to replace the plates I have with these really nice gold-lined plates.
And the mugs and stuff.
So, uh, yeah.
And then just throwing a bunch of other stuff away and organizing.
So, she's starting to get, uh, less of a pack rat.
So, that's good.
So, um, tomorrow, yeah.
Hopefully, maybe I'll be done with 1915.
Let's see.
If I'm done with 1915, uh, map and having put it on the webpage by tomorrow, that'll be a major accomplishment.
Um, I'm kind of nocturnalized.
I crashed at, like, 5.30 or 6 this morning and then getting to get up until, like, 1.
So, I was kind of being pretty good at diurnalization until now.
So, we'll see what happens.
Alright, peace out.
